opening.title: Connect once. Create everywhere.
opening.subtitle: What if every tool you build could tap into one auth layer — your GitHub repos, your Spotify taste, your calendar, your docs — without each tool reinventing OAuth?

the-problem.title: Every app is an auth island.
the-problem.body:
FlowDJ built Spotify OAuth from scratch. Deck Engine needs GitHub tokens. The next tool will need Google Docs access. Each one is a separate auth flow, a separate token store, a separate consent screen.

Users connect the same accounts over and over. Developers build the same OAuth plumbing over and over. Nobody benefits from this fragmentation.

the-insight.title: Threshold already solves this.
the-insight.body: The connect token pattern (thld_ct_) already exists in FlowDJ for Spotify. The same pattern scales to any integration:
bullets: Connect once — user links GitHub/Spotify/Calendar/Notion to their Threshold identity
Apps request access — via connect token, scoped to what they need
User approves per-app — granular consent, revocable anytime
Token management is Threshold's job — apps never see raw OAuth tokens

branch-perspective.title: What's your angle on this?
branch-perspective.subtitle: Different roles care about different parts of this system.

branches.label: I'm building an app
branches.desc: Show me how connect tokens work for my integration

branches.label: I'm a user
branches.desc: Show me what connecting my accounts actually does

branches.label: I'm thinking about the business
branches.desc: Show me why this is a platform play

builder-path.title: For builders: one API call replaces OAuth plumbing.
builder-path.body: Today in Deck Engine, fetching a private repo means building GitHub OAuth, storing tokens, handling refresh. With Threshold:
builder-path.code: // Before: each app builds its own auth const token = await doGitHubOAuth(clientId, secret, scopes); const repos = await fetchWithToken(token, '/user/repos'); // After: ask Threshold for access const gh = await threshold.connect('github', { scopes: ['repo:read'], reason: 'Import repo content for deck generation' }); const repos = await gh.fetch('/user/repos');
bullets: No OAuth client ID/secret management
No token refresh logic
User sees a single consent screen across all Threshold-connected apps
Revocation is centralized — user removes access once, all apps lose it

user-path.title: For users: your vault is your portable context.
user-path.body: Imagine opening Deck Engine and instead of "paste your content" you see "what do you already have connected?" Your GitHub repos, your Notion pages, your Google Docs — all browsable, all available as deck source material.
bullets: Connect GitHub → private repos available as deck sources
Connect Spotify → your taste profile influences deck vibes (future)
Connect Calendar → deck suggests timing for presentations
Connect Notion/Docs → rich source content without copy-paste

platform-path.title: For the business: this is the auth layer that makes everything composable.
platform-path.body: Every app that integrates Threshold connect tokens becomes part of a network. FlowDJ's Spotify taste data could inform Deck Engine's vibe selection. A deck's engagement analytics could feed back into a project dashboard. The more apps connect, the richer each one gets.
bullets: Wedge — FlowDJ and Deck Engine are the first two consumers, proving the pattern
Network effect — each new integration increases the value of connecting to Threshold
Signal protocol — apps don't just consume tokens, they produce signals that other apps can read
Forked generators inherit auth — Sam builds a custom generator, users already have GitHub connected

converge-deckengine.title: What this looks like in Deck Engine, concretely.
converge-deckengine.body: The generator deck's source selection branch gains a new option:
bullets: GitHub (public) — works today, no auth needed
GitHub (private) — Threshold connect token, scoped to repo:read
Paste text — works today, zero friction
Threshold Vault — browse all connected sources: repos, docs, notes, APIs

branch-next.subtitle: This idea is still forming. Your path through this deck is feedback.

branches.label: How does this actually get built?
branches.desc: Implementation sequence, what exists today vs what's new

branches.label: What's the bigger vision?
branches.desc: Deck as thinking tool, not just presentation tool

implementation.title: Build sequence: what exists → what's next.
implementation.body: The pieces are closer than they look:
bullets: Exists now: FlowDJ Spotify connect token flow, Deck Engine GitHub public fetcher, session/feedback persistence to JSONL
Week 1: Generalize connect token pattern in threshold-sdk — threshold.connect(provider, opts)
Week 2: Add GitHub as a Threshold connect provider alongside Spotify
Week 3: Deck Engine generator uses threshold.connect('github') instead of raw API calls
Week 4: Vault browser UI — list connected sources, pick one, pull content

bigger-vision.title: The deck is the thinking medium.
bigger-vision.body:
This deck you're reading right now was the idea. Not a presentation about the idea — the idea itself, structured as branches because the unresolved decisions are branches.

The use case isn't "I need to present." It's "I need to think something through." The flow:
bullets: Have an idea → make a deck. The branching structure forces you to identify decision points.
Share the deck → feedback is path data. Where people drop off = where the idea is weakest.
Refine with feedback → deck refine spec.json "convergence slide loses people"
The refined deck becomes the spec → generate code, plan sprints, write docs from a battle-tested structure.
Threshold provides the integrations → the idea's context comes from your connected sources, not manual assembly.

closing.title: The tool that creates decks is a deck. The feedback on decks is a deck. The idea is the deck.
closing.subtitle: Your path through this deck is already feedback. Hit Escape to save your session, or leave a note.
